Education Assistant Study Page

Building Bridges: Communication, Confidentiality, and Connections

A tutorial page for Day 3 coursework: working with others and families, structured communication, professional boundaries, confidentiality, child-honouring language, and rapport-building with students.

Professional communicationConfidentialityRespectful languagePairingRapport
Original PowerPoint title slide

1. Big Idea: Build Bridges

An EA helps build bridges between students, teachers, families, peers, and the school community. The bridge must be professional, respectful, and confidential.

Bridge activity reflection

When supporting a student with diverse needs, ask: What might be hard? What does the student need to know? What support would make participation possible?

Warm-up: Build a Bridge activity
Memory sentence: Good EA communication is a bridge. It connects people, but it also has safe boundaries.

2. Day 3 Concept Tree

  • Building Bridges
    • Working with others — respect roles, share observations with the teacher, collaborate with the school team.
    • Working with families — families know the child and support learning at home.
    • Home/school communication — structured, objective, teacher-approved communication.
    • Confidentiality — protect information in writing, speech, instruction, assessment, and reporting.
    • Child-honouring language — speak about students with dignity, respect, and belonging.
    • Rapport and pairing — build trust before teaching demands.
    • How to Talk strategies — acknowledge feelings, engage cooperation, encourage autonomy, use descriptive praise.

3. Working With Others and Families

EA responsibilities

  • Respect the roles and contributions of teachers, professionals, parents, and caregivers.
  • Work in partnership with the classroom teacher and school team.
  • Share relevant observations so planning and decision-making are informed.
  • Know your role and stay within it.
Key boundary: EAs support communication, but reporting progress, behaviour concerns, and academic achievement to parents is the teacher’s responsibility.
Working with others
The parent–child–school triangle

Why families matter

Families are part of the student’s learning triangle. They bring knowledge about the child’s strengths, routines, communication, culture, and needs. Family engagement is connected with better attendance, achievement, staying in school longer, and stronger community support.

Hurdles families may face

  • Busy work schedules
  • Language or cultural differences
  • Past negative school experiences
  • Discomfort with school staff
  • Not knowing how to help academically
  • Only hearing from school when there is a problem
  • Educational jargon in reports
  • Childcare needs

4. Home/School Communication

Home/school communication should support two-way sharing, student communication, and consistent support. It should not become a complaint book or an unapproved report.

Home/school communication overview

Three types of home-school books

TypePurpose
Memory bookHelps the student share what happened at school or home. It may use print, voice recording, symbols, or photos.
Homework bookTracks assignments and homework completion, similar to a planner.
Book of highlights and concernsAdults share specific, agreed-upon information. It must be concise and protect confidentiality.
Types of home-school books
Writing frame is better than a blank book

A structured frame helps keep communication brief, objective, and focused. It also helps protect confidentiality and saves teaching time.

5. Objective Written Communication

Use objective language. Write what happened, not your judgment about the student.

SubjectiveObjective
Johnny had a bad day. He wasn’t getting along with peers.Johnny cried and yelled for 10 minutes after recess. He said he was upset about how a peer spoke to him.
He refused to go to gym.He needed support during gym, so he did not make it there today.
Try to avoid labels like: bad day, non-compliant, refused, lazy, manipulative, attention-seeking. Describe the observable behaviour instead.
Objective language example from slides
Practice: rewrite this sentence

Sentence: “She was being difficult and refused to do math.”

Objective rewrite: “During math, she put her pencil down, looked away from the worksheet, and said, ‘I don’t want to do this.’ She completed two questions after a short break and teacher support.”

6. Communication Boundaries With Parents

Teacher responsibility

Communication with parents about student progress, behaviour, and concerns is the teacher’s responsibility. Written communication should be written or initialed by a teacher before it goes home.

EAs should avoid discussing with parents

  • Behaviour concerns
  • Academic achievement
  • Private student information
  • Other students
  • School gossip or staff concerns
Reporting is a teacher responsibility
Cell phone boundaries with parents
Build a relationship with families safely
  • Be friendly, welcoming, and warm.
  • Use small talk.
  • Encourage communication from the student.
  • Talk about general school events or homework tasks.
  • Refer sensitive questions to the teacher.

Course note: Burnaby expectation is that EAs do not communicate with parents by text.

7. Confidentiality

Confidentiality protects student dignity, privacy, human rights, and social inclusion. It applies at every stage of the learning process.

Confidentiality

Information we protect

  • Assessment documents
  • Designations
  • Report cards
  • IEPs and IEP data collection
  • Medical, cognitive, personal, behavioural, and learning information
Think first: Does this person need to know this information to support the student?
At what stages?

Assessing, instructing, evaluating, and reporting. The answer is: at every stage.

Even adapted materials should protect dignity. Use the same title, graphic, font, and paper when possible so students are not singled out.

Confidentiality includes information about learning

8. Verbal Confidentiality

Confidentiality is often broken verbally, not only in writing. Hallways, staffrooms, playgrounds, doorways, and classrooms are risky places for private conversations.

Examples of verbal breaches

  • Saying a student’s designation in a hallway or classroom.
  • Answering “Which student am I here for?” in front of the class.
  • Discussing a student’s progress in a way that reveals private learning levels.
  • Comparing one child to another based on disability-related information.
  • Sharing private information with staff who do not need to know.
Confidentiality related to verbal information
Better phrase: instead of “my student,” say “a student I support” or use the student’s name only when the listener has a legitimate need to know.

9. Respectful and Child-Honouring Language

Words shape how students are seen. Professional language should communicate that every child is liked, respected, capable, and valued.

Why child-honouring language matters
Us vs Them
“Them” language“Us” language
They are obsessed with objects.We like things and have interests.
They are non-compliant.We stand up for what matters to us.
They perseverate.We persevere.
They tantrum.We insist, feel overwhelmed, or need help regulating.
Self-check: Would I say this about any other child? Would I say this about my own child, niece, nephew, sibling, or grandchild?

10. Confidentiality Scenarios

Scenario practice: what would you do?
Scenario A: You want to tell your partner about a difficult day with a student.

Can you use the student’s name and details?

No. Do not use names, age, classroom, or identifying details. Debrief with the classroom teacher or principal if needed.
Scenario B: Another EA asks about a student who stole an eraser.

Should you discuss it in the staffroom?

Only share if the colleague works with the student and needs to know. Ask: Does this negatively reflect the student? Is this necessary for support?
Scenario C: You want to take a photo of student work with your phone and text it to the parent.

Is that appropriate?

No. Use a school device and district-approved communication. Personal devices may not protect confidentiality.
Scenario D: A parent asks, “What’s up with that student?”

What should you say?

Do not share individual student information. Say that student information is confidential and address only the immediate safety or interaction concern.

11. Building Rapport and Pairing

Before instruction, build a working relationship. For many students, especially students with ASD, pairing helps the adult become associated with enjoyable things.

Connections: Building Rapport
Pairing to build rapport
Relationship basics
  • Smile.
  • Listen and take their lead.
  • Find out what they like.
  • Ask them to show you around.
  • Play games.
  • Use interests in learning.
Preference inventory
  • Ask the student.
  • Use choices, ranking, pictures, objects, or surveys.
  • Observe what they choose.
  • Ask teachers and parents.
  • Check the IEP.
Pairing means

Link yourself to preferred objects, activities, outcomes, and situations so the student experiences you as safe, helpful, and fun.

Pairing elements

12. “People Can Be Fun”: Pairing Strategies

1Join what the student starts

Copy or join actions the student already enjoys.

2Create anticipation routines

Use repeated words, gestures, excitement, and predictable first/next sequences.

3Make yourself essential

Set up enjoyable activities that need your help, such as opening a container or pushing a swing.

4Incorporate sounds

Use fun sounds to increase attention, imitation, and shared enjoyment.

5Exaggerate reactions

Use enthusiastic expressions, but adjust if the student withdraws.

6Add surprises

Add small changes to familiar routines to create shared attention and humour.

Join actions
Anticipation routines
Make yourself essential
Incorporate sounds
Add surprises to familiar routines

13. Rapport With Older Students

Pairing with older kids

Older students may need a more subtle, casual approach. Start with short interactions with no expectations. Use fewer words, periodic check-ins, and genuine interest.

  • Get students talking about themselves or their interests.
  • Comment on an object, book, game, or project.
  • Be authentic; avoid over-friendliness.
  • Find a similarity or common ground.
  • Use fun activities, games, humour, Kahoot, Quizlet, or review games.
  • Support peers nearby first if the student is not ready to connect.
No pressure approach: be casual, use no expectations, hum a song, think out loud, look at a book, laugh at their jokes, or use gestures.

14. “How to Talk” Strategies

The strategies from Adele Faber and Elaine Mazlish support communication that respects feelings, increases cooperation, encourages independence, and builds self-esteem.

Four main areas
  1. Helping children deal with their feelings
  2. Engaging a child’s cooperation
  3. Encouraging autonomy
  4. Praise and self-esteem
How to Talk strategy overview
FeelingsAccept feelings
  • Listen fully.
  • Acknowledge with a sound.
  • Name the feeling.
  • Give wishes in fantasy.
CooperationInvite cooperation
  • Describe what you see.
  • Give information.
  • Say it with one word.
  • Describe your feeling.
  • Write a note.
AutonomyBuild independence
  • Offer choices.
  • Respect struggle.
  • Do not ask too many questions.
  • Do not rush to answer.
PraiseUse descriptive praise
  • Describe what you see.
  • Describe what you feel.
  • Sum it up with a word.

15. Practice Responses

Feelings practice
Cooperation practice
Autonomy practice
Praise practice
A student says their laptop will not turn on and keeps talking about it.

Possible response using feelings:

“That is frustrating. You really want it to work.” Then listen, acknowledge, and support problem-solving.
A student spills a drink on a school laptop.

Possible response using cooperation:

“The laptop is wet. Liquids can damage electronics. Paper towels.” Short, calm, specific language works better than a lecture.
A student cannot remember the login password, but it is written in their agenda.

Possible response using autonomy:

“You can check your agenda or try one more time first. I’ll wait.” This respects the student’s struggle and independence.
A student stops chatting and returns to laptop work.

Possible response using descriptive praise:

“You noticed it was work time and got yourself back to the task. That shows self-control.”

16. Quick Review Quiz

1. Who is responsible for reporting student progress and behaviour concerns to parents?
2. “Johnny had a bad day” is...
3. Confidentiality must be protected...
4. Before sharing student information, ask:
5. Pairing is mainly about...
6. “They are non-compliant” can be reframed as...

17. Brightspace Writing Help

Building Connections discussion assignment

Discussion prompt

Question: What are two methods that you find successful to build rapport with students? Give an example of each.

Writing frame

Sample discussion answer

One method I find successful is to start with the student’s interests. For example, if a student likes cars, I might sit nearby, comment on the car, and copy their play before asking for any work. A second method is to use short, casual check-ins with no pressure. For an older student, I might say, “That sticker is cool. Is it from a game?” and then give them space. These methods help build trust because the student experiences the adult as safe and interested, not only as someone who gives demands.

Homework reminder slide

18. Key Takeaways

Professional communication

Be objective, brief, respectful, and teacher-approved when communicating with families.

Confidentiality

Protect student information in writing, speech, instruction, assessment, and reporting.

Rapport

Build connection first. Use interests, play, choices, empathy, and descriptive praise.

One-sentence summary: A strong EA builds connection while protecting dignity, privacy, and professional boundaries.
